If I offended anyone, Mykhailo Albertovych [Fedorov], I'm sorry." He said the issues of military contracts, procurement and mobilisation were a great deal more complicated than most people realised. "Decisions on which people's lives depend are not made with slogans," he said. "Real work, not the public drama surrounding it, wins the war." Protesters will still want to see Fedorov back in a position of real authority, where he can continue to drive forward innovation in Ukraine's battle with Russia. If he gets such a position, Zelensky will have repaired the immediate damage. But many will not forgive him for making what they saw as the wrong choice in the first place.

item.title: Even camels can't cope: Africa's ships of the desert hit by rising temperatures
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cqlxvvw06pqo?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Even camels can't cope: Africa's ships of the desert hit by rising temperatures - Published Camels are famously known as the ships of the desert, but even these hardy animals are not being spared from the extremes of rising temperatures in Africa. The Afar region of north-eastern Ethiopia is one of the hottest and driest places on Earth, where nomads often lead camel caravans carrying salt through the desert. "I can see my camels suffering from extreme heat," Ali Umer, a camel herder from Semera in Afar, told the BBC. "They get blisters on their feet, their eyes become watery and the hot sand burns their skin and eats up their hair when they are sitting," said the 40-year-old who owns around 500 camels. "Within the past one month I have lost eight camel calves because of the extreme heat, it has become much more intense and difficult. "Even the wind, which used to bring cool air, now carries heat," he said, adding he had noticed these drastic changes in the temperature in recent years. He is not alone in his observations: scientists, animal welfare workers and various studies all mention stress, fatigue, dehydration, disease prevalence as direct impacts of extreme heat. The higher temperatures also indirectly make camels suffer by causing severe loss of water, vegetation, and reduced shaded areas. North Africa and the Horn of Africa host around 80% of the world's largest population of dromedary camels - those with one hump. Over the years, farmers and cattle herders in these regions have switched to these animals because of their resilience to harsh heat and drought conditions. But a study based on the perception of camel herders in southern Ethiopia, published in 2025, , externalshowed that high temperatures and climate variability had had a detrimental impact on camel herd health. "Respondents reported that climate change and variability have affected camels' health, productivity, and reproductive performance," it said. In neighbouring Somalia, the situation appears to be more serious. Research published in June, external has shown that drought-related camel mortality is a widespread crisis, affecting nearly 46% of camel-owning households. The study included nearly 6,000 pastoral households and showed that the mortality was concentrated in northern regions, with Sool reporting a "staggering mortality rate of nearly 73%". Extreme heat is the main cause behind the drought in the region although other factors like inadequate veterinary services and animal management make things worse, experts say. An analysis by atmospheric scientists, external found that high temperatures in the region were behind the 2021-22 drought, as extreme heat makes water from soil and water bodies turn into vapour and plants release water in them into air as vapour. Veterinarians say they are treating more sick camels and are seeing more deaths due to extreme heat and drought. "We are seeing significant increase in cases of hyperthermia in camels as a result of which they get multiple infections," Hassan Nuya Guyo, a veterinary officer in Marsabit county in north-eastern Kenya bordering Ethiopia, told the BBC. "In the last two years, we have seen a more than 15% increase in camel deaths in our county due to extreme heat." Disease prevalence among camels and reduced milk yield from them are as a result of climate change impacts including heat stress, according to another study conducted in the Horn of Africa, external. A similar story is playing out in North Africa. A study in Algeria, external, published this year, has shown a significant increase in camel deaths during summer heatwaves. "Time series analysis highlights significant seasonal mortality patterns, underscoring the need for targeted interventions during high-risk periods like peak summer months; such as strategic water provisioning, supplemental feeding, and enhanced disease monitoring," it said. The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) says extreme heat events have become a common phenomenon in several North African countries with heatwaves having increased with a clear upward trend since 1981. It said a number of countries in the region and the Middle East reported temperatures above 50C in 2024. WMO's State of the Climate in Africa 2025 report, external said North Africa recorded the most rapid warming of the six subregions - 0.43C per decade - between 1991 and 2025. Experts say camels comfortably beat the heat up to around 40C by mainly allowing their body temperature to fluctuate by several degrees throughout the day to deal with outside temperature. By doing that they avoid sweating and, combined with concentrated urine and dry faeces, it helps them minimise water loss, while their thick skin coat helps them ward off solar radiation. "But camels can also experience heat stress when temperatures rise beyond their comfort range," said Dr Mohammed Bengoumi, a scientist with the UN's Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), who specialises in camel health and works in North Africa. "In many parts of the Sahara, summer temperatures now frequently exceed 50C, increasing the risk of severe heat stress, particularly when accompanied by reduced vegetation cover, water scarcity, and limited access to shade," he told the BBC. Professor Abdelmadjid Chehma at the University of Ghardaia in Algeria, who has conducted experimental works on camels in the Sahara Desert, has a similar conclusion. "Our works show that prolonged day-and-night heat above 41 to 45C directly attack their cells," he told the BBC. "Their immune system suffers because such heat levels damage their white blood cells and other cells because of stress, they [the cells] struggle to repair themselves and end up destroying themselves." For camels this means when heat stress sets in, their metabolism slows down to limit internal heat production leading to sharp reduction in food intake resulting in lethargy and generalised fatigue. That leads to drop in milk production and also causes reproductive disorders. Professor Mohammed el Khasmi, animal hydrobiology expert at the University of Casablanca in Morocco, has a long list of effects of heat stress he and his team have observed on camels. Weight loss, inflammation, muscle and bone related illnesses, chemical imbalance due to kidney malfunction, infectious diseases caused by parasites were a few among them, he said. While extreme heat is the primary culprit, its impacts become severe when other factors like loss of vegetation and water, lack of shaded areas and inadequate veterinary services add up, experts say. This is why many herders in Ethiopia have moved their camels from the northern region, where the animals were found historically, to the south where water and vegetation are relatively available, experts there say. "And this is not the seasonal movement of the herders," said Daniel Gelan, professor and pastoralist development researcher with Vision University in Ethiopia. "They are leaving the northern region for good because they can no longer sustain camel herding there because of extreme heat and drought," he told the BBC. The Horn of Africa has been recovering from the longest and most severe recorded drought spanning from 2020 to 2023, but now it is seeing another round of the harsh condition, according to charity organisation Oxfam. It said water prices in some parts of Somalia had risen by more than 2,000% because of the drought. Umer, the cattle herder in Afar, said he was worried that an outbreak of disease could start killing his adult camels anytime now. His concern is valid, according to Prof Chehma. "While camels remain the livestock herders' last line of defence against the desert, the unprecedented intensity of current heatwaves directly threatens their vital functions, turning this historically resilient animal into a climate victim," he said. Additional reporting by BBC Amharic's Firehiwot Kassa You may also be interested in: - One boy's fight to save his family from starvation - Published15 November 2022
- Camel milk: Kenya's new 'white gold' Video, 00:01:43 - Published2 February 2021
- The women saving Somalia's animals. Video, 00:01:24 - Published24 May 2017
Go to BBCAfrica.com, external for more news from the African continent. Follow us on Twitter @BBCAfrica, external, on Facebook at BBC Africa, external or on Instagram at bbcafrica, external

item.title: Blocked by censors, China's animal lovers take fight against abuse offline and overseas
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cqx7wd3x420o?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Blocked by censors, China's animal lovers take fight against abuse offline and overseas - Published When videos showing the horrific killing of a stray dog and her puppies sparked outrage on the Chinese internet in June, Beijing's censorship machine kicked into action. But what was unusual this time was that it pitted the shocked and angry voices against authorities who said they were concerned about the four boys involved, all of them younger than 14. China's Communist Party regime tightly controls media and the internet to suppress criticism of the state and to manage public anger. But in this case authorities cited the age of the boys as they urged people to withhold their comments and scrubbed social media posts about the incident. "We urge the public not to disseminate related information, to reject cyberbullying, and to work together to create a good environment for the growth of minors," reads an official statement. But the restrictions only seemed to spur the outcry. China's animal lovers have now taken their fight offline and abroad, even putting up billboards in New York to drum up support. In China, posts about the incident are still drawing thousands of comments across social media, three weeks after the incident. There is shock, anger and sorrow, as well as demands for laws against animal cruelty, which China currently does not have. Disturbing footage showing the boys in the city of Jieyang, Guangdong province, hitting three puppies with sticks before setting their mother on fire emerged online at the end of June. The boys were seen laughing as the dog, named Wang Wang, burned. Jieyang authorities said last month that the boys would not face criminal charges and would instead be sent to a "specialised school", but did not elaborate. Some reports suggest the boys filmed themselves and uploaded the video online, but police have not commented on that. Censors have tried to block the video to protect the boys' identity, although some people argue that it should stay. Their identities have not been revealed, but snippets of the video with their faces visible continue to circulate online. There are also unverified posts claiming to be close-ups of the boys' faces, raising the risk of other children being wrongly identified. Countries around the world restrict information about child offenders or set a high bar for identifying them. For instance, media coverage and public access to juvenile court documents are often restricted to minimise psychological harm caused by undue publicity. In this case, Chinese authorities have not completely banned the discussion – keywords such as "Wang Wang" and "animal protection" still come up as trending search terms. Many are still posting about it, and even raising awareness offline. Still, users have also reported that their posts have been removed, or their ability to share others' posts has been restricted. It is hard to say if the deleted or restricted content had named or identified any of the four boys. Officials have not said anything more on the matter. Posts by several celebrities, including Chinese actor Du Jiang and Taiwanese actress Joe Chen, who had called for China to strengthen protection for animals also disappeared. But it's unclear if these were taken down by the celebrities themselves. Chinese users have refused to back down though, with some turning to advocates abroad to air Wang Wang's plight. It is hard to say why this, of all the sensitive issues that are censored in China, has galvanised such pushback. Part of the reason could be that it's the latest in a string of animal abuse cases that have made headlines recently. And the footage seems to have had a visceral impact. Animal lover PL tells BBC Chinese she found it "affecting in a particularly severe way". "We can't make our voices heard, so many people [outside China] are actively helping us to share this message," the woman in eastern China says. The International Organisation for Animal Protection based in Italy says it has received more than 100 reports from people across the world, calling for the organisation to intervene. It added that it had sought more information from Chinese authorities. In New York City's Times Square, one of the busiest pedestrian intersections in the world, photographs of Wang Wang come up on a gigantic digital screen every 35 minutes. One of the captions reads: "She was loved. She wanted to live. She should still be here." Similar billboards have been put up on a building near Taipei 101, an iconic 101-floor skyscraper in Taiwan. This one includes a picture of Wang Wang nursing her puppies. "This planet is their home too. Justice for Wang Wang," the caption reads. CK, a Taiwanese woman who spent NT$30,000 ($930; £692) putting up the billboard, says she hopes it can foster "a sense of unity". "It makes everyone feel like we're speaking out together on this issue, rather than airing our individual opinions online," she tells BBC Chinese. CK has also set up accounts on platforms like Reddit and X to share the animals' plight. "The world need to know about the cruelty these dogs have been subject to," CK says, adding that she couldn't sleep or eat properly for days after seeing a clip of the brutal killing. Elara, another Taiwanese woman, used AI to translate a summary of the incident into more than 10 languages, such as Japanese, Portuguese and Russian, which have been circulated online. Residents in Hong Kong and Macau have raised funds to put up posters on buses and trains. Within mainland China, the debate continues, with much of the conversation centred around creating awareness to demand laws against animal cruelty. A striking cartoon of Wang Wang and her puppies caught the eye of many football fans during a domestic game at a stadium in Dalian on Saturday. "Every life deserves respect and kindness," read the cartoon that was displayed on a huge screen before the game. Homegrown businesses, such as beauty brand Amortals and streetwear label Wah Yan Ching Nin, have also placed ads outside shopping malls across the country, calling for an end to animal abuse. Stickers and car decals featuring Wang Wang and her puppies have also been put up for sale on China's largest e-commerce platform Taobao. And people have continued to rally online. "Let's keep posting. Don't let the heat of discussion cool down," reads a comment on Weibo. "Constantly speaking up does not mean disturbing social order. [We're doing this] to let more people see it and realise it's such an important thing that we do not mistreat animals," reads another.

item.title: A year after deadly jet crash at Bangladesh school, families demand answers
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cx2j7jgg1z1o?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
A year after deadly jet crash at Bangladesh school, families demand answers - Published It was a normal day for sixth-grade student Nure Jannat Yusha when suddenly, a Bangladeshi air force jet crashed into her school in the capital, Dhaka, killing 36 people - most of them children. Within minutes, Milestone School and College, a private institution buzzing with student activity, was transformed into a scene of chaos and raging flames. It was the deadliest aviation disaster the country had seen in decades. Earlier this week, families and survivors gathered to mark one year since the incident - many of them students who say they are struggling to return to normal life, Yusha among them. "Even now, if I go to school and see a plane, I feel afraid. I can't sleep at night. In my dreams, someone is throwing me into the fire," she told BBC Bengali. She was one of more than 160 people who were injured in the crash at Milestone School - and among those at the memorial held at a hospital in Dhaka on Tuesday, where the families of some victims say they are still waiting for answers about the deadly crash. Yusha's mother Yasin Akter says her child had once dreamt of becoming a dancer - an aspiration that has since faded. "My daughter screams all day and is afraid," she says, adding that Yusha has become paranoid about planes falling from the sky. "Earlier she was interested in studies. Now she does not want to study any more." 'No one can guarantee another plane will not fall' On 21 July 2025, a training aircraft took off from a Bangladesh Air Force Base in Dhaka at around 13:00 local time (07:00 GMT) and crashed soon after in the Uttara neighbourhood. Images from the scene hours after the crash showed scores of emergency service workers sorting through charred rubble in an attempt to find survivors. Authorities announced that an investigation commission had been formed to investigate the crash, but in the days that followed, hundreds of student protesters took to the streets, clashing with police as they demanded accountability over the disaster and changes to training flights regulations. The committee's findings, which it reportedly submitted to the government last November, have not yet been made public. According to a copy of the full report obtained by BBC Bengali, it highlighted issues like Milestone School's lack of compliance with building codes. It also found more than 600 buildings - including schools and hospitals - located near Dhaka's international airport that had violated height restrictions. The report also found that the pilot lacked sufficient flight training and proper ground supervision. And there was no technical fault with the aircraft, the report said - contradicting an early statement by the Bangladeshi Armed Forces that the jet had experienced a mechanical fault after taking off. The Bangladesh Armed Forces did not respond to a request for comment. "No one can give a guarantee that another plane will not fall onto this school in the future," Mansur Helal, the husband of one victim, told the crowd during the memorial, referencing a recent BBC documentary on the committee report. "This is truly a horrific situation. Every day around 2,500-3,000 students go to that school." Helal's wife, schoolteacher Mahreen Chowdhury, had died in hospital from extensive burns she had suffered while rescuing students. "The building in which this accident happened had only one door. How can there be only one door for 800-900 students?" Helal added. Helal and others are calling for the government to release the full report. They are also urging better government support for survivors undergoing treatment, as well as compensation for them and victims' families - which was recommended in the investigative committee report but yet to be implemented. Health Minister Sardar Sakhawat Hossain, who was at the event on Tuesday, said the government would support survivors who require further medical treatment. And Zubaida Rahman, the wife of Prime Minister Tarique Rahman, hailed the courage shown by survivors as "a source of inspiration for the nation". But many of the students who attended the memorial this week, sporting burn scars, are still grappling with their new reality. Mehreen, who's in the fifth grade, says she is no longer able to go to school because of her injuries. "If I get a little sunlight, my whole body burns. For that reason, I do not go to school any more." It has also affected her sleep and learning ability: "Earlier, if I read something, I would memorise it in two minutes. Now I can't memorise a line in two hours. "I can't sleep at night. When I do sleep, I often have strange dreams. I still can't forget that incident." Related topics - Published2 days ago
- Published26 July 2025

item.title: Indian police cracked down on 'cockroach' protesters. They went home and made memes about it
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c3ek3l9gp7go?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Indian police cracked down on 'cockroach' protesters. They went home and made memes about it - Published On Monday, 20-year-old Chhavi (name changed) paused outside a Delhi metro station on her way to what would become one of the largest anti-government demonstrations against Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government in the Indian capital in recent years. She balanced her phone against a railing, stepped back and filmed a quick "fit check" - a video showing off her outfit before the protest. "Going to get beaten by cops, guys," she smiled into the camera. Hours later, police fired tear gas and charged demonstrators with batons as tens of thousands of supporters of the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) – a youth-led movement that grew from protests over exam irregularities into a broader campaign for government accountability – marched towards parliament. They demanded the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over exam paper leaks and recruitment scandals. Pradhan has refused to resign, accusing the opposition of exploiting students for political gain. Authorities suspended mobile internet around the protest site. By evening, protesters said scores had been beaten by police, while police said several officers had also been injured. When Chhavi finally uploaded the videos that evening, one thing stood out. Before the march, she joked about being beaten by police. Hours later, she was still smiling - posing beside riot officers, flashing victory signs, filming through crowds and even interviewing an injured protester. The clips were among thousands of reels, memes and first-person dispatches that flooded Instagram once protesters regained internet access. Some were raw with anger. Others were breathless, frightened or quietly defiant. Yet it was often the sardonic humour that drew the eye. One young woman calmly reapplied her lipstick in front of a line of riot police. Another joked she was "serving face at the protest because the government couldn't serve justice". One reel began as a routine outfit reveal before abruptly cutting to protesters fleeing tear gas. Another gave a deadpan "review" of tear gas, rating its smell and sting like a new restaurant. To many watching online, the humour seemed strangely at odds with the violence. For the young people making the videos, there was no contradiction. None of the protesters interviewed for this story spoke about algorithms, engagement or going viral. They described filming as almost inseparable from protesting itself. And when they turned to humour, it was almost always self-directed: joking about the kilometres logged fleeing baton charges or the absurdity of filming an outfit reveal moments before running into tear gas. The jokes did not erase the fear. They coexisted with it. Experts say the trend hints at a broader shift in how a generation raised online experiences protest. "Older generations separated political life from everyday life," said Joyojeet Pal, a professor at the University of Michigan who studies technology and society. "Younger generations grow up with politics alongside their memes, their family pictures and their daily commutes." For Chhavi, documenting the protest continued once the crackdown began. Some commenters later accused her of treating the violence as content. "But I was adrenaline-soaked, scared, petrified," she said. "It was either laugh in the face of fear or cry." That instinct to keep filming, even as the situation turned violent, is one that Payal Arora, a digital anthropologist at Utrecht University, says has become increasingly common around the world. Smartphones have turned ordinary people into chroniclers of political upheaval, recording moments that might otherwise disappear. "The act of capturing a protest is often an end in itself," she said. "Their message is more like: 'This happened. We were there. We will remember.'" The humour, too, followed a pattern. No young protester described the day as funny. They spoke instead of tear gas, baton charges and the confusion of watching a peaceful march descend into chaos. - India's 'cockroach' protest continues a day after police crackdown - Published1 day ago
- India has a new political superstar - a cockroach - Published21 May
- Why are thousands protesting for education reform in India? - Published14 hours ago
- The hunger-striking engineer demanding education reforms in India - Published2 days ago
People often mistake that humour for detachment, says Nishant Shah, director of Digital Narratives Center at School of Journalism & Communication at the Chinese University of Hong Kong. In reality, he argues, it serves almost the opposite purpose. "Politics is everyday, emotional and experiential," he said. Humour has long been part of protest movements. It can create solidarity, ease tension and help people endure moments of fear. "Even when the cause is brutal and the fight fierce," Shah said, "there is joy, laughter, songs and hope. Without those things, protests don't sustain themselves." Ishaan (name changed), 21, came to Delhi for his first protest. When tear gas landed nearby, he reached for his phone instead of leaving. The reel he posted later showed white smoke drifting across the street beneath a simple caption: "Delhi Police doesn't realise I'm from Jamuna Paar." For most Delhiites, the joke needed little explanation. Jamuna Paar - literally "across the Yamuna" - is shorthand for the city's eastern neighbourhoods and the subject of endless teasing over their neglected civic infrastructure. "The smell there is worse than the tear gas," Ishan said, laughing. He told the BBC the joke was never about pretending nothing had happened. "It was about refusing to let fear dictate the memory of the day," he said. Protest movements have always developed their own emotional vocabulary to help people endure moments of fear together. India's freedom movement had songs and speeches. Trade unions marched behind banners. Student movements embraced posters and street theatre. More recently, the anti-citizenship protests were marked by murals and public readings of the Constitution, while the farmers' movement became synonymous with tractor rallies and livestreams from Delhi's borders. The CJP protests spoke in the idiom of a generation raised online. To dismiss that language as frivolous, Shah argues, is to misunderstand what it is doing. "We often make the mistake of trying to understand new technologies through older ways of thinking," Shah said. But a meme is not simply today's version of a political poster, he said. It gathers meaning as people remake it, parody it and pass it on. Sharing it becomes another way of participating in the protest. That was true for Manit, an 18-year-old engineering student. It was his first protest, and he had almost decided not to film. "I was scared," he said. Then he looked around. "Everyone was recording, talking to the camera, just being themselves. So I asked my friend to record me." At first glance, the reel resembled the workout videos he usually posted. Only the final frame revealed the distance on his fitness app had been logged while fleeing police baton charges. "I escaped the lathi charge," he laughed. "I ran and ran and ended up completing my workout too." For Manit, that was precisely the point. "Not all young people read the news," he said. "If humour can make important conversations accessible, why wouldn't we use it?" *Some names have been changed to protect the identity of the person Follow BBC News India on Instagram, external, YouTube,, external X, external and Facebook, external.

item.title: Watch: Louvre reopens gallery after jewellery heist
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/videos/c1m15l8kgejo?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Watch: Louvre reopens gallery after jewellery heist The Louvre Museum in Paris reopened its Galerie d'Apollon (Gallery of Apollo) to visitors on Wednesday, nine months after jewellery worth millions was stolen in a brazen heist. But the gallery, which once housed France's crown jewels, reopened without any on display. "This is an iconic space within the Louvre, but it will no longer house the Crown Jewels and gemstones, which are to be displayed in secure areas elsewhere in the museum", said the museum's director, Christophe Leribault. The heist took place on 19 October when masked thieves used a truck with a set of mechanical ladders mounted on it to gain access to the gallery via a balcony. Police have arrested four people who prosecutors allege are the thieves - but the stolen items have still not been recovered. item.title: Watch: Beluga whales travel by plane and lorry to US from closed Canadian marine park
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/videos/ckg4x8lpe50o?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Watch: Beluga whales travel by plane and lorry to US from closed Canadian marine park Six beluga whales have arrived at aquariums in the United States after being moved from a closed Canadian marine park that could no longer care for the mammals. They are part of a large pod of 30 belugas living at Marineland of Canada that need to be rehomed. Sierra, Acadia, Osiris and Lillooet arrived at a Chicago facility, while Bertie Botts and Frankie arrived at SeaWorld in San Antonio, Texas. Transported in specialised cold-water containers by truck and plane, the animals were accompanied throughout the journey by marine mammal veterinarians. This is the first stage of an unprecedented international relocation effort. The move followed months of planning and veterinary checks, with specialist teams now monitoring the whales as they settle into their new homes. item.title: Watch Texas police officer pull man from burning car
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/videos/clyej920j5po?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Watch Texas police officer pull man from burning car Bodycam and dashcam footage shows a police officer rescue a man seconds before his car was engulfed in flames near Dallas, Texas. Corporal Joshua Swisher was conducting a traffic stop on a motorway when he was alerted to a nearby vehicle emitting smoke. Shortly after directing the vehicle to the emergency lane, flames erupted from underneath it while the driver was still inside. Newly-released footage shows the incident, which happened on 7 June.
